summary: The 2a/2b split is a natural joint and the group partition largely reflects real dependencies. Both baked architectural decisions (DAG-CBOR+CIDv1 ExecutionState envelope; capability-derived SANDBOX host-function manifest) are sound for the stated cross-phase rationale — they foreclose less than the alternatives and preserve the thin-engine thesis. However, five architectural concerns need addressing before R1 dispatches: (1) the ExecutionState envelope is frozen in 2a but the *payload shape* — specifically whether attribution triples and pinned-subgraph-CIDs are carried through suspension — is deferred to R1 and has cross-phase consequences that make 'R1-level' under-scoped; (2) arch-1's HostError surface decides things Phase 3 sync will need (backend-unavailable vs. storage-conflict discrimination, error round-trip across wire) without that lens being present in G1's review composition; (3) the group dep ordering asserts 'G4+G5 depend on G3' but G4 (Inv-8 + Option-C) is evaluator-structural work with no ExecutionState dependency — this is an artificial serialization that extends the critical path by ~1 day; (4) Inv-13 immutability is correctly double-layered but the plan does not specify the ordering of eval-reject vs. storage-reject firing against version-chain NEXT_VERSION appends (privileged write path), leaving ambiguity at a security-class boundary; (5) Inv-11 'run-time TRANSFORM-computed-CID rejection' names a cost-unbounded check that needs an architectural ceiling before R1.

findings.title: ExecutionState payload shape is load-bearing cross-phase; 'R1-level' scoping under-plays cost
findings.claim: The envelope {schema_version, payload} is decided; the internal ExecutionState struct shape — 'exact field layout (stack snapshot format, context bindings, attribution triple carried through suspension, BLAKE3 hashing boundaries)' — 'is R1-level.'
findings.concern:
Three things the plan treats as payload-shape decisions are actually cross-phase interface decisions that G3-A will freeze on first-commit and every downstream consumer (Phase 2b G6 STREAM, Phase 3 sync, Phase 6 AI forking, Phase 7 Garden approvals) will inherit:

(a) **Attribution triples inside ExecutionFrame.** Invariant 14 (G5-B) requires every TraceStep carry (actor_cid, handler_cid, capability_grant_cid). If WAIT suspends an evaluator mid-walk and the attribution triple is NOT in the serialized frame, then resume-from-bytes cannot re-synthesize attribution for post-resume steps without re-deriving from the resume-side caller — which may be a DIFFERENT actor (Phase 6 AI forking explicitly wants this; Phase 7 Gardens want the opposite: 'vote started by Alice, quorum-reached signal from Bob, but attribution stays Alice'). The ExecutionState MUST carry the attribution triple for Phase 7 to work; it MUST NOT blindly re-use it for Phase 6. This is an architecture decision, not a field layout decision.

(b) **Pinned subgraph CIDs.** ENGINE-SPEC §4 says the evaluator pins the CURRENT version of the running subgraph and all transitively-CALLed subgraphs at walk start. If ExecutionState does not carry these pins, resume-after-crash on a Phase-3-synced instance could see a NEWER CURRENT pointer than the pre-suspension walk — silently violating the version-pinning invariant. This is a correctness issue, not a field layout issue.

(c) **Context bindings with opaque Value.** If context bindings allow arbitrary Value, and Value can contain Cid references, then an ExecutionState's CID is not stable unless context-binding CIDs resolve to fixed content — which they do NOW because Phase-1 is single-instance, but under Phase 3 sync a context-binding CID could resolve to DIFFERENT content on the resume peer. Whether ExecutionState's content-addressing is 'content-addressed over the CID references themselves' or 'content-addressed over resolved content' is a Phase-3-sync decision, not a Phase-2a decision.
findings.cross_phase_impact: Freezing the envelope but deferring the payload is the WORST of both worlds: Phase 2b G6 will compose on an ExecutionState shape that was decided by G3-A's R1 debate under a 2a-only lens. Phase 3 sync will inherit whatever G3-A picked. Phase 6/7 suspension semantics (who 'owns' a resumed workflow's attribution) are quietly decided by G3-A's field layout.
findings.evidence: plan §9.1 last paragraph defers payload to R1; plan §3 G3-A owns exec_state.rs + evaluator.rs serialization; plan §9.1 Rationale bullet 2 explicitly names Phase 6 AI forking and Phase 7 Garden approvals as design motivations BUT does not specify what they need from the payload.
findings.recommendation: Before R1, add a payload-shape sub-decision layer to §9.1 explicitly addressing: (a) attribution triple present-in-frame vs. re-synthesized; (b) pinned-subgraph-CID-set present-in-state vs. re-looked-up on resume; (c) context-binding Value resolution model. At minimum, require R1 to produce a written decision record for each of these three axes before G3-A commits. Add a pre-R1 finding to this effect; add a Phase 6+7 representative lens to G3's R1 review composition (currently chaos-engineer + determinism-verifier + security-auditor — missing a 'future-consumer' lens). Suggest creating or borrowing a 'future-consumer-reviewer' or adding benten-engine-philosophy (who reviewed this in Phase-1 R1).

findings.title: G1 arch-1 refactor freezes the HostError contract without Phase-3 sync lens present
findings.claim: G1 replaces EvalError::Graph(#[from] GraphError) with a new HostError type. §9.2 sketches three options (opaque box + code, enum of known variants, just ErrorCode string). Planner prefers B+A hybrid. G1 review lens is architect-reviewer + benten-engine-philosophy.
findings.concern:
HostError is not just the evaluator's internal error surface — it becomes the contract every Phase-2 primitive (WAIT / STREAM / SUBSCRIBE / SANDBOX) declares its storage-failure shape against. Phase 3 sync then has to wire these errors across a trust boundary:

- A replica that receives a WAIT-resume from a peer whose backend is unreachable needs HostError::BackendUnavailable to be distinguishable from HostError::NotFound, or the resume-then-retry logic conflates 'transient network' with 'this CID was never written.'
- Phase 3's CRDT merge on a conflicting write needs HostError::WriteConflict to be distinguishable from HostError::CapabilityRevoked, or merge picks the wrong conflict-resolution branch.
- Option A (opaque box + code) loses the discrimination Phase 3 needs. Option B (enum) is the correct choice but the *enum shape* needs the Phase 3 lens, which G1's review composition (architect-reviewer + benten-engine-philosophy) does not include.

Also: §5 Rank 8 correctly flags G1 as 15–30 file touch with reviewer-availability risk, but the mitigation ('pair rust-engineer with the implementation developer') doesn't address the lens gap — adding reviewers doesn't help if none of them carry the Phase 3 sync context.
findings.cross_phase_impact: HostError shape gets frozen in G1. Every Phase 2b primitive designs against it. Phase 3 sync, CRDT merge, and cross-peer WAIT-resume all need distinctions that G1's review surface may not surface.
findings.evidence: plan §3 G1 review lens line; §9.2 option sketch; §5 Rank 8 mitigation. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` Layer 2 lists CRDT merge + light-client verification + sync protocol as Phase 3 — all of which consume HostError-shaped failures across a trust boundary.
findings.recommendation: Add a Phase-3-sync lens to G1's R1 review. If no existing agent fits (determinism-verifier is closest; benten-sync does not yet exist as an agent), have the plan author an explicit 'What does Phase 3 need from HostError?' checklist as part of the pre-R1 deliverable and require the checklist resolved before G1-A starts work. Specifically required distinctions: BackendUnavailable vs. NotFound vs. WriteConflict vs. CapabilityRevoked vs. Panicked. Serialize-across-wire requirements (DAG-CBOR round-trip) should be stated now even if not implemented in 2a.

findings.title: Group dep ordering claims 'G4+G5 depend on G3' but G4 has no ExecutionState dependency
findings.location: plan §6.Stage parallelism line 489 + §3 G3, G4, G5 dependency listings
findings.claim: 'G4 + G5 depend on G3. G6 depends on G3 + G4. G7 depends on G3 + G5 + G6 (longest pole).'
findings.concern:
G4 is Inv-8 multiplicative + Option-C evaluator-path. Its files are invariants.rs + read.rs + call.rs + iterate.rs. None of these touch ExecutionState or WAIT — G4's work is frame-counting at registration time (static multiplicative budget) and capability-check threading at execute-time on READ. The only G3 output G4 needs is the stable host surface from G1 (which G3 also consumes). So G4's true dependency is G1, not G3.

Similarly G5-A (Inv-11, Inv-13 eval side) does not need ExecutionState. Only G5-B (Inv-14 attribution onto TraceStep) has a potential overlap with G3 — and that overlap is exactly the payload-shape concern in arch-1 above.

Consequence: the critical path currently is G1 → G3 → G4 → G6 (Phase-1-style) with G3 and G7/G8 bulging out. If G4 can run in parallel with G3 (both depend only on G1), the 2a wall-clock shrinks by ~1 day. The plan's §7 calendar assumes G4 after G3 — this is the source of the artificial serialization.
findings.cross_phase_impact: Phase-2a only (no cross-phase leak), but the dep misstatement also hides which invariant work really needs WAIT's exec-state shape. For an R1 critic to push back on 'does G4 need G3?' the plan needs to state the real reason, not a conservative catch-all.
findings.evidence: plan §3 G4: files_owned `invariants.rs` + `primitives/{read,call,iterate}.rs`; plan §3 G3: files_owned `exec_state.rs` + `evaluator.rs` + `primitives/wait.rs`. The file sets overlap on evaluator.rs (G3 touches suspend/resume; G5-B touches attribution-on-TraceStep) but NOT with G4.
findings.recommendation: Correct §6.Stage parallelism to: 'G1 strictly first. G2 + G3 + G4 + G9 can run in parallel (all depend only on G1). G5-B may serialize after G3 (attribution threading through evaluator.rs overlaps suspend/resume threading through evaluator.rs). G5-A runs in parallel with G3. G11 runs last.' Update §7 calendar. Flag that ONLY G5-B and (later) G6 need G3 output; everything else just needs G1.

findings.title: Inv-13 immutability double-layer correctly chosen but firing-order for privileged writes is unspecified
findings.claim: 'Double-layer: eval prevents the write attempt; storage is the backstop.' Storage side: 'Reject put_node when the target CID already exists unless WriteContext::privileged is true (engine API path for version-chain NEXT_VERSION append).'
findings.concern:
Double-layer is the right architectural call. But the plan does not specify what happens when:

(a) A user subgraph's WRITE primitive targets a CID that exists as a version-chain Anchor. Evaluator sees a registered-subgraph re-write attempt → rejects. Storage never sees it. Fine.

(b) Engine's internal version-chain append path calls put_node for a NEW Version Node that hashes to a pre-existing CID (content collision on a deduplicated node). With WriteContext::privileged=true the storage layer accepts, but the content is ALREADY in place — this is deduplication, not immutability violation. Correct behavior is no-op-return-existing-CID, not reject.

(c) Engine's grant-write path (capability grants are system-zone, always privileged) puts a Node whose CID already exists. Same as (b): dedup.

The plan's storage-side rule 'reject put_node when CID already exists unless privileged' treats privileged writes as a BYPASS of immutability rather than as a deduplication-friendly path. This conflates 'immutability' (you cannot change what a CID resolves to — which is free because content-addressing guarantees it) with 'single-write' (you cannot re-put the same CID — which is a different property).

BLAKE3 + DAG-CBOR guarantees property 1 (immutability) for free. The plan is really implementing property 2 (single-write / first-wins) as a DoS / consistency guard. These should be named differently and the privileged-bypass rule should be re-examined: is it actually 'privileged paths perform upsert (no-op if CID matches), user paths reject on CID existence'? That's a cleaner architecture.
findings.cross_phase_impact: Phase 3 sync explicitly exchanges version Nodes (ARCHITECTURE.md §'Cross-instance sync'). Every received version Node will hit put_node on the receiving peer; many will be dedup hits. If the storage-side rule rejects ALL re-puts (privileged or otherwise) the sync protocol will either have to pre-check existence (extra round-trip) or carry a 'dedup-expected' flag. If the rule upserts-when-content-matches, Phase 3 gets natural dedup.
findings.evidence: plan §2.2 G4 description; plan §2.5 E11; plan §5 Rank 7 fallback. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` §'Cross-instance sync' on version Node exchange.
findings.recommendation:
Rename the storage-side check to 'single-write' or 'collision-guard' and define the firing matrix explicitly:

| caller | CID exists, content matches | CID exists, content differs |
|---|---|---|
| user subgraph | ??? | reject E_INV_IMMUTABILITY |
| engine privileged | no-op OK | reject (content-address violation) |

R1 should fill in the `???` — 'no-op OK (dedup)' is the Phase-3-friendly choice; 'reject' is defense-in-depth. The 'content differs' case in the privileged path CAN'T happen under honest writers because content-addressing guarantees CID = hash(content); if it happens the storage is corrupt and the right action is a hard panic, not a typed error. State this explicitly.

findings.title: Inv-11 runtime 'TRANSFORM-computed-CID rejection' has no cost ceiling
findings.claim: 'Registration-time check for static-literal CIDs, at run-time for dynamically-computed CIDs.' Rank 6 fallback: 'O(1) HashMap probe per primitive call against a system-zone prefix table.'
findings.concern:
A HashMap probe per primitive call is 'O(1) with a high constant' — if the primitive is inside an ITERATE body at 10k iterations, the cumulative cost is 10k probes per handler invocation. For a handler that does 5 READs inside a 10k ITERATE, the probe cost is ~50k HashMap lookups per call. On top of the capability check at commit + the attribution threading + the frame accounting for Inv-8, the runtime invariant stack is getting fat.

The plan does not say whether the system-zone prefix table is a static CONST (compile-time) or a runtime Node lookup. If the former, probe cost is genuinely small. If the latter, the probe becomes a mini-READ and the performance target of ≤300µs for a 10-node handler is at risk.

Also: the check runs 'against a system-zone prefix table' — but the invariant is 'system-zone LABELS unreachable.' Labels are Node properties, not CIDs. A TRANSFORM-computed CID has no label until the target Node is fetched. So the check is either:
- Fetch the Node, read its label, check prefix. Cost: full read per primitive.
- Maintain a system-zone CID index, probe it directly. Cost: cheap but needs invariant-maintenance on grant/view/manifest creation.

The plan picks neither.
findings.cross_phase_impact: The cost ceiling decision affects the ENGINE-SPEC §14.6 performance target. If runtime Inv-11 enforcement forces a Node fetch, the '150–300µs 10-node handler' gate 5 becomes unreachable and the perf regression is attributable to 2a, not to something 2b will fix.
findings.evidence: plan §2.5 E10 target text; plan §5 Rank 6 fallback text. ENGINE-SPEC §14.6 10-node handler target.
findings.recommendation: R1 must decide the prefix-table shape. Recommend: maintain a system-zone CID index (stored in the system zone itself) updated atomically with every system-zone Node creation. Invariant-11 runtime check becomes a single redb get_or_default lookup — ~0.5µs at hot cache, bounded by backend latency cold. State the index-maintenance obligation as part of the grant-write + view-create + manifest-install paths. Update perf gate 5's 300µs target to carry the Inv-11 probe in the accounting.

findings.title: G5-B attribution threading and G3 exec-state serialization share evaluator.rs — serialization order unspecified
findings.claim: G3-A owns `crates/benten-eval/src/evaluator.rs` (serialise-on-suspend, restore-on-resume). G5-B owns `crates/benten-eval/src/evaluator.rs` (attribution threading onto every TraceStep).
findings.concern:
Both groups write to evaluator.rs. If G3-A commits the suspend/resume threading and G5-B commits the attribution threading in parallel, the second committer rebases against the first's shape. If G5-B commits first, G3-A's serialize-state discovers it needs to carry attribution in the frame (arch-1 finding). If G3-A commits first, G5-B's attribution threading needs to know the frame shape (which fields participate in suspension).

The plan's stage parallelism (line 489) says 'G4+G5 depend on G3' — which implies G5 after G3 — but G5-A (Inv-11, Inv-13) could run in parallel with G3, and G5-B (attribution) should NOT be parallel with G3 for exactly this reason.
findings.evidence: plan §3 per-group files_owned maps; plan §6 line 489.
findings.recommendation: Split G5 into G5-A (Inv-11 + Inv-13, parallel with G3) and G5-B (Inv-14, serial after G3). State the evaluator.rs ownership hand-off explicitly: G3-A lands first; G5-B rebases on top and threads attribution through both the walk-phase and the suspend/resume-phase of evaluator.rs. Failure to sequence will cause merge-conflict churn on the most-touched source file in 2a.

findings.title: SANDBOX cap-derived manifest needs an explicit namespace owner before 2b opens
findings.claim: Host functions declare 'requires: host:<domain>:<action>' scope. 'Named-manifest registry shape — TOML in the workspace, or a benten-caps-adjacent module? R1 decision.'
findings.concern:
The decision is architecturally correct (single security model, attenuation works, revocation works) but the plan defers the namespace-owner question to R1. That decision actually has cross-phase consequences:

- If host:<domain>:<action> lives in benten-caps, every new host function requires a benten-caps PR — tight gate but couples host-fn vocabulary to the caps crate's release cycle.
- If it lives in a TOML catalog in the workspace, the catalog is not code — it cannot participate in the content-addressed subgraph world (docs/PROJECT-HISTORY.md: 'every other Benten artifact is content-addressed').
- If it lives as subgraphs in the system zone (option C-style, the Phase-1 thesis applied to its conclusion), then host-function registration becomes a runtime capability grant rather than a compile-time fixture. This is the thin-engine-pure answer but needs Inv-11 system-zone + module manifest support (G10) — so it's 2b-coupled.

This is the same architectural consistency question arch-1 surfaced for ExecutionState: the Benten thesis wants every artifact content-addressed. A TOML file in the repo is an outlier.
findings.cross_phase_impact: Phase 2b G7 (SANDBOX) needs the namespace to be decided before host functions ship. Phase 8 Credits compute marketplace needs third-party host-fn registration — which only works if the namespace is extensible at runtime (i.e., content-addressed, not TOML). Picking TOML now forecloses Phase 8's extensibility story.
findings.evidence: plan §9.3 last paragraph; 2b outline §5 second bullet. FULL-ROADMAP Phase 8 compute marketplace requirement 'third-party host-fn caps.'
findings.recommendation: R1 should tentatively pick 'host-function registry = subgraphs in system zone' with a 2b pre-R1 review. Failing that, pick TOML but state the Phase 8 migration plan ('host-function vocabulary migrates from TOML to system-zone subgraphs before compute marketplace opens'). Do not leave the decision to G7's R1 six months from now when the 2a-frozen cap namespace has already shipped.

findings.title: G7 scope-split writeup still references G3+G4+G5+G6 deps but G6 moved to 2b — G7 dep list stale
findings.claim: 'G7 depends on G3 + G5 + G6 (longest pole).' Stage parallelism line repeats this.
findings.concern:
G6 is now [PHASE 2B]. G7 is also [PHASE 2B]. Within 2b, G7's dependency on G6 may still hold (SANDBOX-streaming-output is a STREAM-composing pattern), but the plan's dependency arrow still reads as if all groups were in a single phase. Small issue — just a stale editing artifact from the 2a/2b split.

Also: 2b opens its own pre-R1 which will re-examine these dependencies. The plan correctly says 2b's scope is 'frozen shape' to carry forward, but dependencies might change when 2b's R1 lens (wasmtime-sandbox-auditor + ivm-algorithm-b-reviewer) sees the real constraints.
findings.recommendation: Split §6 stage parallelism into '2a-only parallelism' (G1 → G2+G3+G4+G9 → G5 → G11) and '2b-forward-look parallelism' (carry forward, explicitly 'subject to 2b pre-R1 re-examination'). Minor cleanup.

executionstate_format.reasoning: The envelope choice (DAG-CBOR + CIDv1) is architecturally correct and the cross-phase rationale (Phase 3 sync symmetry, Phase 6 AI forking, Phase 7 Garden approvals) is real. Options B (borsh) and C (JSON) both foreclose more than they save: JSON regresses determinism (ENGINE-SPEC §7), borsh introduces a second canonical format the content-hash infrastructure doesn't understand. The versioned envelope (schema_version: u8) correctly addresses forward-compat. Caveats: the payload-shape sub-decisions (attribution triple presence, pinned-subgraph-CID set, context-binding Value resolution model) are load-bearing cross-phase interfaces being treated as 'R1-level' internal details — see arch-1. Address those before G3-A dispatches and this decision is fully sound.

sandbox_manifest_architecture.reasoning:
Capability-derived host-function manifest is the architecturally correct choice. The reasoning in §9.3 holds up:

- Benten already has one security model (UCAN-compatible caps with pluggable policies). A parallel allowlist would duplicate TOCTOU / revocation / attenuation machinery — the exact duplication §9.3 identifies and the Compromise #1 batch-boundary refresh shape already solves.
- Phase 8 compute marketplace genuinely requires fine-grained, user-grantable host-fn caps (docs/future/compute-marketplace.md, FULL-ROADMAP Phase 8). A tiered system cannot express 'this agent can call host:compute:time + host:net:outbound-to-host-X but nothing else.' Cap-derived can.
- CALL attenuation naturally extends to SANDBOX (Phase 1 CALL semantics already attenuate on isolated=true). Hand-allowlist cannot attenuate.

Named-manifest DX sugar is orthogonal to the security model (it's a lookup table over cap bundles). Sound.

Residual concerns are implementation-level (arch-7 on namespace owner; §9.3 open questions on initial host-fn set and enforcement layer) — not architecture-level. Sound-as-architecture.

scope_split_assessment.reasoning:
The 2a/2b split at the 'evaluator-structural work vs. isolation-and-compute work' line is a natural joint. The review-lens non-overlap argument (§8) is real: code-as-graph-reviewer + invariant lens vs. wasmtime-sandbox-auditor + ivm-algorithm-b-reviewer genuinely don't share expertise. Blast-radius isolation (2a well-known vs. 2b unknown-unknowns on wasmtime API) is real.

All 2a items share: 'evaluator surface work that every 2b item composes on.' All 2b items share: 'isolation / compute / distributed build target work.' Nothing is forced across the line:

- WAIT (G3) is 2a — correct. WAIT's ExecutionState shape is load-bearing for G6 STREAM, so it MUST land in 2a so G6 has a frozen shape to compose on. (The arch-1 concern is about the payload-shape *decision process*, not about the group placement.)
- Inv-8 multiplicative (G4) is 2a — correct. Lands cleanly without SANDBOX because SANDBOX fuel is orthogonal to ITERATE/CALL iteration budget (fuel is instruction count inside wasmtime; iteration budget is frame count outside wasmtime). The multiplicative through CALL accounting is a static registration-time check against declared `max` on ITERATE nodes — it doesn't need SANDBOX's runtime fuel meter.
- Algorithm B (G8) is 2b — correct despite view_stale_count being 2a. The plan correctly absorbs view_stale_count into G11 (a 10-line wire-up), isolating the full Algorithm B surface to 2b where ivm-algorithm-b-reviewer leads the R1 lens.
- G2 durability default + AST cache is 2a — correct. These are perf debt-close items with no isolation surface; putting them in 2b would dilute the SANDBOX-lens council.

The one joint-adjacent item that could have crossed wrong but didn't: SUBSCRIBE. The Phase-1 internal change-stream IS in 2a (change-stream plumbing for G11 metric wire-up); the user-visible SUBSCRIBE primitive is in 2b (composes STREAM's chunk_sink). Correct split — the internal plumbing is a change-notification subsystem, the user primitive is a stream composition.

group_partition_review:
Six 2a groups (G1, G2, G3, G4, G5, G9, G11) is about right in count but the dep ordering is slightly wrong — see arch-3 and arch-6. Corrected ordering: G1 (serial, first) → G2 + G3 + G4 + G9 (parallel, all depend only on G1) → G5-A (parallel with G3; Inv-11 + Inv-13) + G5-B (serial after G3; attribution threading shares evaluator.rs with G3 suspend/resume) → G11 (docs + devserver + view_stale_count + TS wait() example, depends on G3 landed). This shrinks the 2a critical path by ~1 day. No additional groups needed; no group needs splitting beyond the G5-A/G5-B separation in arch-6.

One additional observation: G11 carrying view_stale_count + TS wait() DSL example + missing_docs sweep-for-2a-items + devserver is a broad grab-bag. If G11 time-slips, the devserver (T2, developer-facing) gets sacrificed to the docs sweep (E13). Consider splitting G11 into G11-A (docs + view_stale_count wire-up) and G11-B (devserver + DSL example refresh for wait()) — this gives dx-optimizer a coherent lens on G11-B while documentation-engineer leads G11-A. Not critical for 2a shipping; minor refinement.

The scope cut into 2b (G6, G7, G8, G10, plus a 2b-only G11 equivalent) reads correctly and defers the design-heavy items to 2b's own pre-R1. No group from 2b should migrate back to 2a.
cross_phase_interface_risks: ExecutionState payload shape (attribution triple carried through suspension, pinned-subgraph-CID set, context-binding Value resolution model) frozen in 2a G3-A without a Phase 3 / 6 / 7 lens at the R1 review. Consumers: G6 STREAM (2b), Phase 3 sync cross-peer WAIT-resume, Phase 6 AI assistant workflow forking, Phase 7 Garden approval flow signatures. Mitigation in arch-1.

HostError enum shape (replacing EvalError::Graph) frozen in 2a G1 without a Phase 3 sync lens. Phase 3 CRDT merge + cross-peer replica error-reporting need specific discriminations (BackendUnavailable vs. NotFound vs. WriteConflict vs. CapabilityRevoked). Mitigation in arch-2.

Inv-13 immutability storage-side rule 'reject put_node when CID exists unless privileged' frozen in 2a G2-A conflates immutability (free from content-addressing) with single-write / collision-guard. Phase 3 sync de-duplication on received version Nodes will hit this rule. If the rule is 'upsert-when-content-matches' on privileged path, sync is natural; if 'reject all re-puts', sync needs a pre-check round-trip. Mitigation in arch-4.

Inv-11 runtime TRANSFORM-computed-CID check lacks an architectural cost ceiling. If R1 picks 'fetch-node-and-check-label' the 300µs 10-node handler perf gate (exit criterion 5) may not close. If R1 picks 'system-zone CID index' the index-maintenance obligation extends to every grant-write + view-create + manifest-install path — a cross-cutting refactor that must be stated now. Mitigation in arch-5.

SANDBOX host-fn namespace owner (TOML catalog vs. benten-caps module vs. system-zone subgraphs) deferred to 2b R1, but the cap-namespace host:<domain>:<action> is baked in 2a-level decisions today. If R1 eventually picks 'system-zone subgraphs' the Phase-2a cap-string-format has already shipped — backward-compat migration becomes the cost. Mitigation in arch-7.

Subgraph AST cache (G2-B) freezes the cache-key derivation shape. If the key is just the subgraph CID, Phase 3 sync's version-chain-CURRENT-moves-on-receipt case silently invalidates caches; correct, cheap. If the key is (subgraph CID + capability-context hash) as Inv-13 + Option C might suggest, Phase 3 needs the capability-context to be content-addressable. Plan does not specify. Minor — flag at R1.

WriteContext::privileged is re-used in both the Inv-11 registration-literal-CID rejection exception AND the Inv-13 storage-side re-put exception. This single boolean is doing work for two distinct invariant relaxations. If Phase 3 adds a third (sync-replica writes on behalf of another peer), the boolean becomes a capability-shaped concept. R1 should consider lifting WriteContext::privileged to an enum now rather than retrofitting later.
